summary:
The normal growth of the maxilla happens via apposition where new bone tissue forms specifically at certain locations; this results in its advancement forward, downward, and outward without simply moving existing bones around. This matches well with definition provided for 'apposition' related to growing structures like craniofacial regions such as the maxilla during development stages up until adulthood when skeletal maturity sets in fully completing all necessary expansions needed throughout life span milestones including puberty among others impacting overall facial morphology significantly over time due largely but not exclusively from processes involving appositional additions contributing substantially towards final adult form seen clinically today post-adolescence completion phase marking end stage developmental changes occurring naturally within human anatomy framework governing these critical transitions crucial understanding contextually here regarding patient care needs assessments etc...
